possible_slide_title: Why Not Just Use an MLP for Images?
visible_text:
Why Not Just Use an MLP for Images?
Example: 32×32×3 image → 3072 inputs
Hidden layer (1000 neurons) → 3 million parameters
Problems:
Too many parameters
No spatial structure
MLP ignores 2D geometry and local relationships.
No translation invariance
Shifted objects are treated as new patterns.
Overfitting risk
Millions of parameters relative to dataset size.

How CNNs Differ from MLP

Why CNNs Work for Images
Exploit local structure
Share filters across space
Translation equivariant (feature maps shift when input shifts).
Pooling → approximate invariance
Exploit local structure

What Is Inductive Bias?
Inductive bias:
The assumptions a model makes about the structure of data before seeing any training examples.
All models have inductive bias.
Linear regression → assumes linear relationship
MLP → assumes no spatial structure
CNN → assumes local correlations and translation consistency
Images are structured 2D signals:
Local correlation
Repeated patterns (edges, textures)
Spatial hierarchy
CNNs encode spatial inductive bias through:
Local connectivity
Weight sharing
Translation consistency
This reduces parameters and improves generalization.
43
Good generalization happens when the model’s inductive bias matches the true structure of the data.
If data is images → spatial bias helps
If data is time series → temporal bias helps

44
Hierarchical Feature Learning in CNNs

1×1 Convolution
What Does 1×1 Convolution Do?
Performs channel mixing
Earlier layers detect: Edges, Textures, Colors, Simple patterns
1×1 convolution learns how to combine them.
Example: Combine “horizontal edge” + “skin color” → “face feature”
So, it learns interactions between feature maps.
Reduces dimensionality
Less computation
Less memory
Lower overfitting risk
Adds non-linearity
Performs linear projection
Followed by non-linear activation
Enables bottleneck architectures (e.g.,
ResNet
).

Pooling: Max vs Average
Max Pooling
Average Pooling
Focus
Strongest activation
Overall activation level
Edge preservation
Preserves sharp features
Smooths features
Noise sensitivity
Can amplify noise spikes
More stable to noise
Information retention
Keeps strongest signal only
Keeps more global information
Common usage
Most common in early CNNs
Often used in final layers
Intuition
Keep the strongest evidence
Capture overall presence.
When to Use Which?
Early feature extraction → Max pooling
Final feature summarization → Global average pooling
Small datasets → Average pooling can help reduce overfitting

Key Takeaways
Convolution = local dot product
Filters are shared across space
Depth grows with number of filters
Padding and stride control output size
CNNs scale to large image datasets
Modern vision breakthroughs stem from CNN architectures
CNNs encode spatial inductive bias for visual data.
